did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid AI method combining a Convolutional Neural Network (ResNet-50) for feature extraction and the Wildebeest Herd Optimization (WHO) algorithm for feature selection to predict and diagnose Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) skin cancer using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the development of a new hybrid algorithm (RWHO) combining ResNet-50 and Wildebeest Herd Optimization for predicting basal cell carcinoma. The paper includes specific sections for 'Materials and Methodology', 'Dataset Used', 'Observations/Results', and 'Conclusion', and reports original performance metrics (e.g., 94% accuracy) on the HAM10000 dataset, which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: RWHO: A hybrid of CNN architecture and optimization algorithm to predict basal cell carcinoma skin cancer in dermoscopic images
brief_summary: This study proposes RWHO, a hybrid algorithm combining ResNet-50 for feature extraction and the Wildebeest Herd Optimization (WHO) algorithm for feature selection to predict basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from dermoscopic images. The method was evaluated using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains over 10,000 images of various skin lesions. The performance was assessed using accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and F1-score, comparing the proposed model against state-of-the-art optimization algorithms like Chimp Optimization Algorithm (ChOA) and Biogeography-Based Optimizer (BBO). The RWHO algorithm achieved a superior accuracy of 94% and sensitivity of 94%, outperforming comparative methods such as Dorj’s (88%) and Linsangan’s (82%) approaches. Additionally, the proposed method demonstrated reduced execution time and effective feature dimensionality reduction. The results indicate that RWHO is a highly efficient and accurate computer-aided diagnosis system for early BCC detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study; the RWHO algorithm was specifically designed and evaluated to predict and diagnose BCC skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) skin cancer diagnosis (binary: BCC vs. non-BCC)
all_task_types.details: The paper predicts and diagnoses Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The model uses ResNet-50 for feature extraction and Wildebeest Herd Optimization (WHO) for feature selection to classify images. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics: Accuracy (94%), Sensitivity (94%), Specificity (91%), F1-score, PPV, and NPV. The task involves distinguishing BCC lesions from other skin lesion types present in the dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images. A public dataset combining dermatoscopic image sources from various people around the world, containing a variety of skin lesion categories.

use.training: Used for feature extraction via ResNet-50 and feature selection via WHO algorithm.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the proposed RWHO algorithm against state-of-the-art optimization algorithms.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed RWHO algorithm and state-of-the-art methods were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, etc.) and fitness values without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The RWHO algorithm achieved the highest accuracy (94%) and sensitivity (94%) among the compared methods. The fitness values for feature selection were also compared: WHO had an average fit value of 0.032, best fit value of 0.036, and worst fit value of 0.049, outperforming ChOA (Avg: 0.210), BBO (Avg: 0.588), and LS (Avg: 0.081). The proposed method also demonstrated reduced execution time compared to state-of-the-art algorithms.

architecture_used.main_model: RWHO (ResNet-50 combined with Wildebeest Herd Optimization)
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 pre-trained on the ImageNet database (trained on more than a million images, categorizing images into 1000 different item categories)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: ResNet-50 used to extract initial features (feature maps) from dermoscopic images
architecture_details.optimization_component: Wildebeest Herd Optimization (WHO) algorithm used for feature selection to reduce time complexity and eliminate useless features
architecture_details.hybrid_mechanism: The output features from ResNet-50 are fed into the WHO algorithm to select beneficial features. The WHO algorithm iterates 40 times with 40 candidate solutions.

training_details.training_schedule: The WHO algorithm was iterated 40 times. All algorithms were executed for 40 independent executions to produce consistent results.

classification_or_output_head.details: The system uses the optimized features selected by WHO for classification. Specific classifier head architecture (e.g., SVM, Softmax) is not explicitly detailed, but performance is evaluated using Accuracy, Specificity, Sensitivity, F1-score, PPV, NPV, and Dice Coefficient.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: RWHO (ResNet-50 combined with Wildebeest Herd Optimization)
preprocessing_techniques: Input images were pre-processed to meet the requirements of the ResNet-50 model.
conclusions: The proposed RWHO algorithm, which combines ResNet-50 for feature extraction and the Wildebeest Herd Optimization (WHO) algorithm for feature selection, achieved a superior accuracy of 94% and sensitivity of 94% in predicting basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from dermoscopic images.

The RWHO algorithm outperformed state-of-the-art optimization algorithms (Chimp Optimization Algorithm, Biogeography-Based Optimizer, and Locust Swarm Optimization) in terms of fitness values for feature selection, demonstrating better efficiency in selecting useful features and eliminating irrelevant ones.
The proposed method demonstrated reduced execution time compared to state-of-the-art algorithms, indicating improved computational efficiency.
The RWHO algorithm achieved higher Positive Predictive Value (PPV) and Negative Predictive Value (NPV) compared to comparative methods, indicating a higher likelihood of correctly predicting BCC.
The study confirms that the hybrid approach of using a CNN for feature extraction and a metaheuristic optimization algorithm for feature selection is effective for early detection of skin cancer.

The proposed method achieved the highest accuracy among compared techniques, surpassing Dorj’s method (88%), Linsangan’s method (82%), Khan’s method (81%), Thanh’s method (79%), and Angurana’s method (73%).
important_remarks: The abstract explicitly states the method was tested using the PH2 dataset, but the main text, dataset section, and results consistently refer to the HAM10000 dataset; this discrepancy between the abstract and the body of the paper is a significant reporting error.

The study claims to use the HAM10000 dataset (which contains 7 classes including melanoma, nevus, etc.) but focuses exclusively on Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) without clarifying if it is a binary classification task (BCC vs. non-BCC) or multi-class, nor does it report class distribution or handling of class imbalance.

The paper compares the proposed RWHO algorithm against other methods (Dorj et al., Linsangan et al., etc.) using metrics from those cited papers rather than re-running their models on the same data split, which prevents a fair, controlled comparison and introduces potential bias from differing experimental setups.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported for the performance differences between the proposed RWHO algorithm and the comparative methods.

The paper does not report the specific train/validation/test split ratios used for the HAM10000 dataset, nor does it describe the cross-validation strategy, raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to a specific split.

The specific classifier head used after feature selection by the WHO algorithm (e.g., SVM, Random Forest, Softmax) is not explicitly detailed, only that 'feature selection based classification' is performed.
The study relies on a single public dataset (HAM10000) without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report the number of features extracted by ResNet-50 or the final number of features selected by the WHO algorithm, making it difficult to assess the extent of dimensionality reduction achieved.
